data.title: Arquitos Capital Q4 2025 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Partner,

Arquitos returned 82.1% net of fees and expenses in 2025, compared to 12.8% for the Russell 2000. Individual returns will vary based on the timing of investment. Please check your statement for specific results.

In 2025, Arquitos Capital completed its best year in its 14-year history. We have returned higher than 40% in six of those 14 years.

One year ago, I wrote that I was as confident in the portfolio as I have ever been. While I am pleased by the performance last year, I also believe that our portfolio companies may be just as undervalued today as they were one year ago.

We are primarily concentrated in our top three positions: Liquidia Corporation, ENDI Corp, and Finch Therapeutics. We also have a handful of small positions and some cash. I have been looking at a variety of potential new investments in order to diversify the portfolio, but the good news is that I have not found any that offer a more compelling risk/reward profile than our current holdings.

As a reminder, Arquitos is an unconventional hedge fund invested in a small number of unique companies. I look for company-specific situations where the potential reward meaningfully outweighs the risk. I want multi-bagger potential with limited downside risk.

I am looking for situations where there is a misunderstanding by the market. Typically, this occurs in companies that are going through transitions. There may be structural or strategic changes within the company, including reorganizations, spin-offs, or some other catalyst that is being unlocked. Significant misunderstandings happen more often in smaller companies, so most of my focus is in that area.

These opportunities are fairly rare. That is why the portfolio is concentrated. It can sometimes take years for the public to understand what is happening at the company or for the transition to fully occur. Because of that, short-term performance is not particularly relevant. A company can trade for a depressed price for an extended time period and then have its stock price dramatically increase over a short amount of time once the markets have more clarity.

I have written about this in the past and feel very strongly that we have a significant advantage being comfortable with uncertainty if the downside is protected by a strong balance sheet. Investors love certainty and will pay a premium for it. But often we have opportunities where extremely positive things are happening at a company behind the scenes, but it takes several quarters or years for the income statement to show it. By investing early in those situations, we can capture tremendous upside prior to other shareholders buying in. Then, we can get another leg up in the stock price when those new, more income statement-oriented investors enter.

I have applied this strategy to our three largest positions below:

Liquidia Corporation (LQDA)

Liquidia was our largest contributor in 2025. Shares ended the year at $34.49, increasing from $11.76 at the start of the year. We primarily own long-dated call options that are in the money.

We first bought Liquidia in October 2022 at $4.83 per share. At the time, the company was solely a legal special situation play. Prior to our entry, Liquidia had lost a case at the federal district court level involving the '793 patent owned by competitor United Therapeutics (UTC). However, the Patent Trial and Appeals Board (PTAB) then found that the '793 patent was invalid. This district court decision had been handed down prior to the PTAB decision.

This created an interesting dynamic and extremely attractive entry point. The next step was a PTAB appeal, which Liquidia won. Then, of course, the district court decision had to be overturned since you cannot violate a patent that is not valid.

Since then, we have had several other legal impediments involving Liquidia, UTC, and the Federal Drug Administration. Eventually, in late May 2025, Liquidia’s medication, Yutrepia, finally came to market.

This situation was a sweet spot for the Arquitos strategy. My ideal situation is entering an investment that is a special situation. Due to the uncertainty and complexity, there is a higher chance that the stock is mispriced. Then, when the special situation aspect is resolved and the company performs operationally, a new set of investors who are focused on the income statement begin to invest. This transition involving turnover into different types of investors can create significant opportunities.

Strict special situation investors exit with a nice gain after the legal situation is done, but they miss out on the additional gains (and potential future buyout) that come later. Income statement or growth investors buy from the special situation investors once there is more clarity. These investors missed out on the initial gains but benefit from the longer-term operational success of a company.

My ideal situation is to capture both the special situation gains and the longer-term gains.

Liquidia fit this approach well. Their launch may go down as one of the most successful drug launches ever. They have already taken 25% market share and are effectively expanding the market. Amazingly, 75% of all patients starting on Yutrepia are new to treprostinil.

As a reminder, treprostinil is the generic drug delivered either through inhaled methods, oral, via IV or subcutaneous. Liquidia’s treatment method is more effective than their competitor's because of Liquidia’s patented PRINT manufacturing process. PRINT allows the treprostinil powder to be smaller and in a more uniform shape, allowing the drug to reach the deep lung in patients. This both increases effectiveness and reduces side effects like cough. Many patients have a very difficult time breathing, and Yutrepia’s low effort inhaler is much easier for them. The competitor’s manufacturing process does not allow for the use of a low-effort inhaler.

Other delivery methods for treprostinil carry their own side effects and are used at different stages of disease progression. It is generally better for the patient to be on the inhaled treatment as long as side effects are lower and high enough doses of treprostinil can be delivered to the deep lung. This is why clinical studies involving Yutrepia show how effective it is for patients. Yutrepia can deliver higher doses with fewer side effects. Liquidia should be able to capture, and already are capturing, the inhaled market, naïve patient market, and some of those patients on other treatment regimens.

Specific to the inhaled market, CEO Roger Jeffs believes that Liquidia can take 80% to 90% of UTC’s market share. Liquidia’s Q4 results show that this is beginning to happen. Liquidia generated $30 million in positive free cash flow in Q4 2025 on $90 million in revenue. That number will exponentially rise from here.

The final significant legal issue is a case where UTC alleged that Liquidia violated UTC’s ‘327 patent. That patent generally covers improving exercise capacity in patients suffering from PH-ILD. This patent and case do not involve the treatment of patients suffering from PAH.

The trial was in late June 2025. We are expecting a decision any day. The outcome could range from a full win by Liquidia to a full removal of Liquidia’s ability to have PH-ILD listed on Yutrepia’s labeling. There also could be potential outcomes in between, such as some sort of royalty involving PH-ILD prescriptions. Liquidia is confident in a full win, but anything can happen in litigation. Many legal observers believe that the longer it has taken for the judge to provide his decision, the better it is for Liquidia.

Importantly, Liquidia has stated that 75% of their revenue so far is from PAH prescriptions. So, even in a worst-case scenario involving this litigation, the company’s potential is still strong. We are likely to see some volatility, either positive or negative, depending on the outcome. If the decision goes against Liquidia, I believe the share price decline would be short-lived, as their PAH business alone supports a higher stock price than today.

The company recently presented at the JP Morgan healthcare conference on January 14. They provided details on the launch of Yutrepia, the potential for their next generation nebulizer, L606, and a host of new clinical trials to show that Yutrepia can also effectively treat Pulmonary Fibrosis, Systemic Sclerosis associated with Raynaud’s Phenomenon, and COPD. These markets dwarf the current PAH and PH-ILD market that Liquidia participates in.

In the second half of 2026, Liquidia is participating in an investigator-initiated study to confirm the effectiveness of combining sotatercept with treprostinil for better patient outcomes. Sotatercept is an injection that reduces lung vessel pressure, while inhaled treprostinil reduces long vessel resistance. This combination therapy has the potential to become the gold standard for PAH patients. The commercial name for sotatercept is Winrevair. It is owned by Merck. Merck has been active in the M&A space, and many believe that they would be a logical buyer of Liquidia.

At the JP Morgan conference, Roger Jeffs said that he believed Liquidia would reach $1 billion in revenue in 2027. I believe that number is conservative. In early 2024, I said that I thought Liquidia’s base case was $75 per share by 2027, with a bull case of $100 per share. If the company generates the $1 billion in revenue that Roger Jeffs believes, shares should trade above that bull case number.

ENDI Corp. (ENDI)

One year ago, I wrote that ENDI was the best near-term risk/reward opportunity since I launched Arquitos Capital. Shares began 2025 trading at $11.43, which was less than 5x EBITDA net of cash and investments. Assets Under Management (AUM) were at $3.4 billion, and the annual EBITDA run rate was $8 million. ENDI’s stock was cheap on its own and did not give credit for any future growth. The chances of losing money on an investment with those characteristics and at that price were minuscule.

The stock rose to $16.75 by year-end. The EBITDA run rate hit $12.5 million annually, and AUM stood at $4.1 billion. Using the last publicly reported financial numbers, ENDI continues to trade around 5x EBITDA. Similar specialized asset managers trade at valuations far above that number.

A major overhang for the stock was the outstanding warrants at the company level. These were issued when ENDI acquired CrossingBridge. The strike price was $8 per share. The accounting treatment for these warrants made the income statement confusing and especially difficult for new potential shareholders analyzing the company for the first time.

The company did an early cashless exercise of these warrants (at a discount) prior to the end of 2025. This means that the financial statements in the future will be more clear and the company will be easier to analyze. Now that the market cap is above $100 million and the structure is cleaner, I expect the company will begin to attract additional interest and become more fairly valued over time.

Finch Therapeutics (FNCH)

Finch shares ended 2025 at $13.49, rising by 19% for the year despite no major updates to their court case.

Finch won a jury trial in August 2024 against Ferring Pharmaceuticals, with the jury finding that Ferring infringed on three of Finch’s patents. The jury awarded Finch approximately $30 million in a one-time licensing fee and pre-trial interest, plus ongoing royalties to be determined by the judge and post-trial interest. Importantly, the jury also found willful infringement, opening the door for enhanced damages up to three times the jury award.

We continue to wait for the post-trial decision from the judge, where she will determine an ongoing royalty rate and determine whether enhanced damages will be awarded. My guess is that part of the delay is that the parties are attempting to negotiate a settlement. Patience is our friend here, as interest on the judgement accrues at the Federal Reserve discount rate plus 5%.

I expect our administrator to deliver the K-1s in late March.

For our audit, you may receive communications and verification requests from Wipfli as they perform their work. The sooner you complete and return these items, the sooner we can receive the fund’s final audit. I appreciate your cooperation. If you have any questions about any of these items, please let me know.

Thank you again for your commitment to Arquitos, and best wishes for 2026!

Best regards,

Steven Kiel

Disclaimer

This letter is for informational purposes only and does not reflect all of the positions bought, sold, or held by Arquitos Capital Offshore Master, Ltd. or its feeder funds, Arquitos Capital Partners, LP, and Arquitos Capital Offshore, Ltd. Any performance data is historical in nature and is not an indication of future results. All investments involve risk, including the loss of principal. We disclaim any duty to provide updates or changes to the information contained in this letter.

Performance returns presented above are for Arquitos Capital Partners, LP and reflect the fund’s total return, net of fees and expenses, since its April 10, 2012, inception. They are net of the high water mark and the 20% performance fee, applied after a 4% hurdle, as detailed in the confidential private offering memorandum. Arquitos Capital Offshore, Ltd. was launched on March 1, 2018. Returns from Arquitos Capital Offshore, Ltd. may differ slightly and are not presented here.

Performance returns for 2025 are estimated by our third-party administrator, pending the year-end audit. Actual returns may differ from the returns presented. Positions reflected in this letter do not represent all the positions held, purchased, or sold.

This letter in no way constitutes an offer or solicitation to buy an interest in Arquitos Capital Partners, LP, Arquitos Capital Offshore, Ltd., or any of Arquitos Capital Management’s other funds or affiliates. Such an offer may only be made pursuant to the delivery of an approved confidential private offering memorandum to an investor.

data.title: Gold Rally Hits $4,900: Why The January FOMC, Geopolitics, And Technical Warnings Mark A Critical Juncture
data.description:
By Moheb Hanna

The historic rally in gold prices, which saw the metal surge past $4,900 per ounce in late 2025 and early 2026, was driven by a rare convergence of macroeconomic and geopolitical factors. With the January 27-28, 2026 FOMC meeting approaching, the backdrop of high-altitude stability for gold is in focus. Prices have already surged significantly - trading near $4,800-5,000 - driven by a combination of fiscal expansion, geopolitical friction, and institutional uncertainty. The January meeting is less about a "rate move" and more about the "narrative shift."

How this meeting is poised to impact gold prices

The "Pause" and interest rate path

According to the CME FedWatch tool, the futures markets are pricing in just under two 25 bps interest rate cuts for 2026, with the cut expectations mostly for the second half of 2026. Lower nominal rates reduce the "opportunity cost" of holding gold, which pays no interest, making it more competitive against Treasury bonds.

For the January 27-28, 2026, FOMC meeting, the market consensus is that the Fed will hold rates steady at 3.50-3.75% after a string of cuts in late 2025. The CME FedWatch tool has 95% of participants expecting the Fed to hold, meaning a pause is largely priced in. However, gold’s sensitivity to the "Neutral Rate" remains the key variable. If Chair Powell’s press conference suggests that 3.5% is the new floor (the terminal rate) due to resilient growth, gold could see a short-term "profit-taking" pullback. Conversely, if the Fed expresses concern over the cooling labor market (currently adding only 50k jobs/month), markets will pull forward expectations for a June cut, providing a tailwind for non-yielding bullion.

Inflation vs. fiscal policy

Concerns around stagflation dominate the 2026 economic landscape. Inflation remains stubbornly above the 2% target, remaining near 2.7-2.8%, while expansionary fiscal policies and tax cuts are propping up growth.

Historically, gold has thrived when the Fed was perceived as "behind the curve." If the FOMC statement acknowledges that inflation progress has stalled but refuses to hike rates (due to growth risks), it reinforces gold's status as the ultimate inflation hedge.

Institutional independence and geopolitical risk

Unusually for an FOMC meeting, non-monetary factors are heavily influencing the "Fed premium" in gold prices. The recent legal and political challenges to the Fed’s independence (including the Supreme Court's scrutiny of Governor Lisa Cook's removal and investigations into Chair Powell) have fueled concerns about the Fed’s independence and its potential impact on future interest rates. Investors are using gold as a hedge against a potential "politicization" of the dollar. Any rhetoric in the January 28 statement that hints at defensiveness toward the Fed’s autonomy could actually spike gold prices as a vote of no confidence in the USD. Major banks (Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan) have already raised 2026 targets to $5,000-5,400, suggesting that as long as the Fed doesn't pivot back to aggressive hiking, the path of least resistance for gold remains upward.

With all that said, will this price rally continue? We will go over different technical analysis tools and indicators, as well as the most recent COT report released on January 16th, 2026, including data up to January 13, 2026

Commitments of Traders (COT) Report

Managed money (Large speculators): Trend followers

Unlike previous peaks where speculators were "overextended," the current net-long level is not yet at a historical sentiment extreme. Although this suggests there is more room to the upside regarding long positions, and they haven't yet reached the "exhaustion" point that usually precedes a major crash, a negative divergence may be in play as price makes higher highs while long positions are plotting lower highs.

Swap dealers: Market makers and hedgers

In contrast to the managed money category, swap dealers, who typically take the other side of the market, have already reached their all-time extreme short level, suggesting a change in sentiment may be due.

​Small speculators: Growing participation

There is a noticeable uptick in net long positions from small speculators in early 2026. This indicates that "Main Street" investors are increasingly entering the market, often in the final stage of a significant price surge.

Open interest and volume

Although open interest remains high, it is showing a potential negative divergence with price action. Similar to OI, non-trending low volume is also seen along with price appreciation as of November 1, 2025, which theoretically means that volume was not supportive of the price increase since November 1.

Technical analysis: Gold daily chart - Bullish momentum testing resistance

  • The daily chart shows a strong, long-term bullish trend that accelerated significantly in 2026. After a period of consolidation in late 2025, the price has broken out to the upside, currently trading near 4,900.00.
  • As of early 2025, the gold price traded within a widening, rising channel, as indicated by the blue lines on the chart above. The "higher highs and higher lows" structure is well-defined, with the upper channel boundary intersecting with monthly R3 at 4913.58, forming a confluence of resistance.
  • As of late October 2025, following a negative divergence with the RSI that preceded a $200 drop, gold prices rose back up, erasing the drop, breaking above its fast EMA9, the monthly PP of $4345.22, and the baseline of multiple higher lows, as marked by the red lines on the chart.
  • A confluence of support lies beneath the price action, represented by the intersection of the EMA9, the breakout level (red line), and the newly formed price gap near $4630.00. The price gap may suggest an island formation; however, that’s yet to be determined.
  • RSI (14) is currently at 78.00, indicating the asset is in overbought territory. A potential negative divergence remains between price and RSI.
  • ​Stochastic (14, 1, 3): The Stochastic oscillator is also highly elevated (87.90), confirming strong bullish momentum but suggesting that a short-term “cooling-off” period or minor retracement toward the EMA may be imminent.

data.title: ENFR: My Choice In The Midstream, And Some Drivers Not To Underestimate
data.description:
When I think of a sector that has regained momentum, I think of midstream energy, and among the ETFs that try to represent it, personally, I really appreciate Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR).

I notice a strong relationship between the performance of the ETF’s holdings and credit/monetary dynamics, almost more than energy ones. The reason? In my opinion, its composition made up of C-corps and MLP entails steady cash flows but pronounced costs of capital. If the latter declines, then the market can also allow itself to expand company valuations. But before getting into these details …

What is ENFR

We are talking about a fund with a primary objective of price + yield of the index and as a secondary objective, total return (income + capital appreciation). To do this, ENFR replicates the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index (AMEI), an index that cannot exactly be defined as a “pure MLP ETF.” Unlike classic indices such as the Alerian MLP Index, here there is a strong presence of C-Corps (such as Kinder Morgan, Enbridge, etc.). This makes ENFR more of a midstream hybrid: in detail, it has an MLP limit of 25% of total assets; this means that about 75% is in C-corps.

Participating involves an expense ratio of 0.35% (very low for the category it belongs to) and a median bid/ask spread of 0.19%, with a relatively contained turnover of 33%. Since we are talking about costs, it makes sense to remember that ENFR is an RIC (Regulated Investment Company), which (at least at a theoretical level) entails lower tax drag and greater NAV retention. Which (again theoretically) places it on an interesting level for taxable accounts and IRAs.

Distribution

The fund has a quarterly distribution, currently corresponding to a TTM distribution of 4.53% with a 30-day SEC yield of 5.04%. A distribution that is not as pronounced as classic MLPs but still steady and linear (an element that I personally appreciate a lot). All of this (naturally) with a very high Return of Capital of ~53.3% of the TTM distribution (also justified by its tax nature).

This is also partly due to the business model of the companies that make up the ETF, which is based on fee-based contracts and relatively stable cash flows. In fact, let’s look specifically at which companies we are talking about.

Holding Distribution

We are talking about 27 companies, with a strong concentration in the top 10 holdings equal to 60% of a total capitalization of $755 bn.

Of this distribution of holdings, about 71% is in the USA, the rest in Canada, all large cap (total absence of small cap). At a sector level, we are talking about companies active in the North American energy infrastructure segment, but with market characteristics close to those of utilities. As you well know, there is a tight connection here.

This clearly results in a hidden concentration on Natural Gas, which, in my opinion, cannot be completely ignored (gas pipelines and gathering & process), close to 60%. A commodity by which it is naturally (partly) influenced, especially in short-term fluctuations.

It also has a 28% exposure to oil pipelines, which seems to be scaring some investors a bit due to new bearish expectations on the price of oil, but which, in my opinion, is not that influential.

Peers

ENFR has a beta practically equal to 1 versus the AMEI index, but it has an alpha of -0.6 due to cost frictions and replication techniques that increase tracking error. However, it remains very representative in terms of the beta of the segment it aims to represent.

In this sense, the comparison with the MLP Index is mandatory: here the beta versus MLP drops to 0.75. And moving to the alpha side, two elements stand out in my opinion: a lower upside capture by ENFR, equal to 90, but a downside capture of 72. So, in this sense, it is a more conservative solution. From here derives the comparison with AMLP, which uses the MLP index as a benchmark.

At first glance, an important difference in the TER immediately stands out, with ENFR clearly more cost-effective. Going further into detail, a divergence in distributions also emerges: by reducing exposure to the MLP component, ENFR ends up giving up part of the income stream. It is precisely from here that, in my view, the performance gap that emerged in the period following 2022 originates. Businesses that are heavily asset-heavy and highly sensitive to the cost of capital, refinancing dynamics, and credit spreads tend to suffer during phases of financial tightening. ENFR, with a greater presence of C-corps, therefore showed greater weakness in a credit-tightening environment such as the post-2022 period. If this interpretation is correct, then it is natural to expect that in a phase of credit stabilization, ENFR can gradually close the gap relative to AMLP. A dynamic that has indeed already manifested itself quite clearly starting from 2023.

My Opinion

After the end of the Chevron doctrine + a long series of Trump’s executive orders, the sector’s focus has shifted from revenues to the implicit cost of capital. This, together with credit and monetary dynamics, acts on the denominator (WACC) and seems to allow a justified expansion of valuation. This, considering long, predictable, contract-based cash flows in a context of ever-increasing energy demand.

How to Position

Personally, I therefore remain positive on this sector, and I look with interest at this phase of potential accumulation that started around $30. Being a sector with positive momentum, I orient myself using the 14-period RSI strategy on the 30 level, and in ENFR I see a potential controlled-risk trade: the fund distributes more than an average 4% per year and has a POC around the $30 area about 12% away (to the downside).

Therefore, considering the positive impact of the compression in the cost of capital, prospectively also supported by liquidity injections into the credit system by Trump, and the prospects of Fed cuts, the market could price in a further reduction in the cost of capital on ENFR’s holdings, which, with FCF unchanged, would further raise the aggregate target price.

For this reason, I remain bullish on ENFR at price lows (technical side).

Risk

I want to clarify one thing: in my opinion, attention should go beyond concerns about gas or oil in recent months. The risk develops in two directions:

  • Regulatory dynamics are fragile: they change depending on consensus. Therefore, in my opinion, they are not such strong drivers in the long term.
  • Stable, or moderately high, real rates for a long time still; and considering that even today the market is pricing in high yields, this means that market participants do not strongly believe in the idea of new rate cuts, or in any case they expect higher inflation in the future as a result of this decision. And if this direction were to continue, the thesis that justifies an expansion of target prices, making growth less sustainable, would fall apart.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think the right rating for ENFR is BUY. In my opinion, it has a structure that revolves around an index built in a competitive way compared even to the classic MLP. I am not concerned by the fact that distributions are halved, nor that 75% is made up of C-corps. I think credit prospects can have a positive impact and leave room for an expansion in ENFR’s valuations. For this reason, I still look with interest at the lows.

data.title: Canadian REIT Capital Offerings Up Over 23% In 2025
data.description:
Editor's note: This article is published quarterly with current data available at that time.

Publicly traded Canadian real estate investment trusts raised C$6.23 billion through capital offerings in 2025, an increase of more than 23% from the C$5.03 billion collected a year earlier.

Debt offerings accounted for C$5.44 billion of capital raised, and common equity offerings brought in about C$785.7 million.

Retail REITs account for 60% of total capital

Six property sectors in Canada raised capital in 2025. The retail sector, comprising shopping centers and other retail properties, accounted for the biggest share at C$3.77 billion, or more than 60% of the total for the year.

The office segment came in second, collecting C$850.0 million, or 13.7% of total capital raised. The multifamily sector ranked third at C$561.9 million, about 9.0% of the total.

The healthcare and industrial sectors collected C$500.0 million each, while the diversified sector raised C$40.3 million.

Allied Properties pulls in the most capital

The single largest common equity offering in 2025 was multifamily-focused GO Residential REIT's (GO.U) C$561.9 million IPO completed July 25. The REIT used the net proceeds from the offering in large part to fund the acquisition of the initial portfolio.

Office landlord Allied Properties REIT (APYRF) attracted the most capital overall in 2025 through three debt offerings totaling C$850.0 million. Its C$450.0 million offering, which wrapped up Feb. 24, 2025, was the largest debt issuance completed during the year. Part of the offering's net proceeds were intended to finance or refinance eligible green projects and fully repay the construction loan on 19 Duncan St. in Toronto.

Allied Properties' two other offerings were completed April 7, 2025: C$250.0 million of 4.312% series M senior unsecured debentures and C$150.0 million of floating rate series L senior unsecured debentures. Both series of debentures are due April 7, 2027.

Other retail REIT Choice Properties REIT (PPRQF) and shopping center REITs SmartCentres REIT (CWYUF) and First Capital REIT (FCXXF) all tied for the second spot, each raising C$800.0 million in 2025 through debt offerings. Shopping center-focused RioCan REIT (RIOCF) followed next with three debt offerings totaling C$750.0 million.

Capital offerings up over 12% sequentially in Q4 2025

Following a jump in the third quarter, capital market activity continued to increase during the fourth quarter of 2025, bringing in C$1.42 billion for Canadian REITs. The total represented a hike of more than 12% from the previous quarter; however, it was a drop of more than 16% from the C$1.70 billion raised during the fourth quarter of 2024.

During the fourth quarter of 2025, shopping center REIT Primaris REIT (PMREF) raised C$168.9 million through a follow-on common equity offering. The REIT said it would use the proceeds to pay down debt relating to the recently completed acquisition of Promenades Saint-Bruno.

Single-tenant-focused Automotive Properties REIT (APPTF) completed a C$38.9 million follow-on common equity offering Oct. 23, 2025. The retail REIT said it intended to use a portion of the proceeds to repay indebtedness under its credit facilities, including the debt incurred to fund the cash portion of the acquisition of three automotive dealership properties in Dorval, Quebec.

Automotive Properties collected C$16.0 million through another common equity offering also completed Oct. 23.

SmartCentres and First Capital pulled in the biggest amounts of capital during the fourth quarter, both at C$500.0 million.

During the fourth quarter, SmartCentres sold $250.0 million of 3.599% series AC senior unsecured debentures due June 12, 2029, and $250.0 million of 4.318% series AD senior unsecured debentures due June 12, 2032. It intended to use the net proceeds to refinance existing debt and for general corporate purposes.

First Capital raised C$250.0 million through its 4.760% series G senior unsecured debentures due Feb. 15, 2035. The shopping REIT used the proceeds to repay, in part, the early redemption of C$300 million of outstanding co-issued 3.604% series T senior unsecured debentures due May 6, 2026, at the end of December 2025. The REIT also collected another C$250.0 million through its 4.461% series F senior unsecured debentures due Feb. 15, 2034, and intended to use the proceeds to repay existing debt and for general business purposes.

Meanwhile, RioCan sold C$200.0 million of 4.417% series AP senior unsecured debentures due Oct. 1, 2032. The shopping center REIT said it would use the net proceeds for debt repayment and general business purposes.

data.title: TE Connectivity: A Booming AI Business Lifts Spirits
data.description:
In the spring of last year, I concluded that TE Connectivity (TEL) was resorting to M&A to improve its growth and positioning following a +$2 billion deal for Richards Manufacturing.

The focus on M&A came as the company had not really lived up to its promises for sales growth, even as the positioning of the business is generally fine, and TE has a strong long-term track record. While valuations look very reasonable at a high-teens earnings multiple, I was not too impressed with ongoing growth, making me cautious.

This stance has been too cautious, as shares have risen by nearly 60% over the past year. This has been supported by strong business momentum as well as a substantial re-rating, as organic growth improved meaningfully, notably thanks to an exploding AI business.

However, part of the gains were driven by a valuation re-rating, for good reasons. With the booming AI-related business making up just about 15% of growth and the remainder of the business posting much more muted growth, I am remaining cautious, waiting for better entry points.

All About Connection

TE Connectivity describes its business as a leading industrial technology business that focuses on a +$100 billion connectivity market. The company generates some $17 billion in revenues from such solutions, with revenues split pretty evenly between the industrial and transportation segments.

Here, the company provides connectivity and sensor solutions used to create a safer, sustainable, and connected future. Such solutions are provided in quite harsh environments, including digital data networks, energy, aerospace & defense, automated & connected living, medical, automotive, sensor, and commercial transportation.

The company has operations spanning over a hundred countries, with over a hundred manufacturing sites present around the globe as well. This provides real diversification on top of diversification founded in end markets, with the company being a large business, employing over 80,000 employees.

The company has quite a solid track record, having grown sales from about $11 billion to $18 billion over the past decade, although that revenue flatlined around $16 billion in the period 2022-2024. GAAP operating margins improved quite a few points, up to levels around 20% of sales, as another big achievement has been 20% of the shares being bought back over this period of time.

A $60 stock a decade ago rose to the $150 mark last spring, as shares have been on fire over the past year, having risen to a high of $250 per share, now trading at $230 per share.

Some Perspective

As alluded to before, the situation around TE was not that bright early last year. This came after the company reported a 1% fall in fiscal 2024 sales to $15.8 billion, driven by general weakness. Despite this weakness, earnings improved, with adjusted earnings reported at $7.56 per share, as a net debt load of $2.9 billion was pretty manageable.

Trading at $150 in March, shares traded at 19-20 times earnings, with few immediate green shoots seen. The company posted flattish first-quarter results. Valued at $48 billion at $150 per share, the company announced a substantial bolt-on deal with a $2.3 billion deal for Richards Manufacturing, a rather expensive deal valued at 6 times sales, while all of TE traded around 3 times sales.

Pro forma net debt was seen increasing above $5 billion, yet I pegged leverage ratios only around 1.5 times EBITDA. Working with an $8 per share number, a resulting 18-19 times forward earnings multiple looks pretty reasonable for a fine and modestly leveraged business, as real execution seemed to be lacking here.

Improvements Arrive

In March, TE Connectivity reported a 9% increase in the quarterly dividend, now payable at $0.71 per share each quarter. Soon momentum improved, with second-quarter organic growth accelerating to 5%, as the order intake was up as much as 6%.

Third-quarter sales rose by 14%, driven by the Richards deal, yet a 9% organic growth rate should not be underestimated as well. This momentum only accelerated with fourth-quarter sales reported up as much as 17%, with organic growth accelerating to 11%.

Strong momentum resulted in full-year sales reported up 9% to $17.3 billion, with organic growth up 6%. However, the momentum was really different this time, with transportation solution sales down around a percent for the year, while industrial sales were up over 23%. This, in its turn, was driven by digital data networks and energy markets.

All this drove some margin gains as well, with adjusted earnings up a dollar and twenty cents to $8.76 per share. Net debt was down to $4.4 billion, driven by retained earnings since the Richard deal, with leverage far from being an issue.

With this trend firmly in place, the company guided for another 17% increase in first-quarter sales, with ongoing growth still seen in the double digits, increasing by around 11%.

Just Keeps Going

In January, the company reported a 22% increase in first-quarter sales to $4.7 billion, with organic growth of 15% outpacing the outlook by around four points. Adjusted earnings rose by a third to $2.72 per share, with earnings trending around $11 per share. Moreover, continued momentum is seen in a $5.1 billion order intake, adding about $400 million to a non-quantified backlog, as net debt is pretty stable at $4.4 billion amidst continued buybacks.

The growth acceleration comes as transportation solutions are now seeing solid growth as well, while industrial growth approaches 40% on an annual basis. This, in its turn, is driven by nearly 70% growth in the digital data network business, which is now a nearly $3 billion business on an annual basis.

The company guided for slower growth, in part because of tougher comparables, with second-quarter sales seen up 13%, driven by 6% organic growth. Anticipated adjusted earnings of $2.65 per share are actually seen down a few pennies on a sequential basis. This likely is the reason shares have been trading stagnantly since the fall, in fact, down about 8% from the highs.

What Now?

Trading at $231 per share and earnings power having jumped from about $8 per share to about $11 per share, TE has seen dramatic earnings improvements as well as a re-rating in the share price. The earnings multiple has jumped from 18-19 times last year to about 22 times here, as the company has been unleashed again.

Frankly, the results are impressive, with organic sales increasing by double-digit percentages, but the fact that this was flat just a year ago shows the volatility herein and reliance on the AI boom to drive growth here. Moreover, the transportation business remains a dominant business, one that I have lower regard for, as the re-rating is really driven by the industrial businesses and, in particular, the AI business.

Frankly, the company has delivered, driven by the AI boom, but this growth is more widespread. Shares are not necessarily super expensive, but a shortfall in this business, or somewhat poorer-positioned transportation business, makes me cautious here, as expectations have risen substantially.

With the second quarter guidance softening, which very well might turn out to be conservative, the issue is that I am not willing to chase shares here, certainly as the growth guidance is softening a bit. This makes me cautiously optimistic, and I am not yet willing to get involved. However, upon further developments or a pullback to the $200 mark, I am pleased to revisit this cautious yet constructive stance.

data.title: 3M: The Hard Part Is Over, But The 'Easy' Money Is Gone
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

3M Company (MMM) delivered an impressive recovery from their lows in 2023-2024, with the market appreciating the new CEO’s turnaround efforts, although I believe most of that has already been rewarded, and the current price doesn’t offer a good enough opportunity anymore, trading around what I’d call a fair value.

In 2025, 3M reported adjusted free cash flow of $4.374 billion, although it’s important to note that 3M uses this number as a way to show their “real” potential by adding back the significant litigation expenses that I’ll mention below.

This is a bit better than the $4.322 billion expected by S&P Global during their March 2025 report, reinforcing the already above-expectations performance delivered by 3M’s new management since the new CEO took the role in May 2024.

What’s also in line with S&P’s expectations is the 2026 guidance of $5.6 billion to $5.8 billion in adjusted operating cash flow (S&P sees $5.8 billion in CFO in 2026), guiding for ~$1.1 billion in CAPEX, with ~4% total sales growth (~3% adjusted) and a 70-80 bps operating income margin expansion.

Overall, they see 2025 as a year that further rebuilt their foundation, while expecting to outperform the macro environment in 2026 (which is expected to see improvements in some areas), with 3M being either ahead or on track to meet their medium-term commitments so far.

Financially, based on 3M’s latest report, we see an overall solid position now, with the current assets well above the current liabilities, although the long-term debt and other liabilities are also significant, even though they are actively repaying them.

Very importantly, we see a ton of cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, which can offer a safety net in the face of upcoming legal liabilities.

Dividends & Buybacks

3M used to be a relatively high-yield dividend payer (a Dividend King with a 64-year streak), but that had to change due to the Solventum spin-off and the significant pressure the company was facing especially as a result of the lawsuits/settlements, and it took a CEO change to announce something like this (previous CEO announced it, new one executed it), doing a “clean slate” maneuver to avoid putting this kind of blame on the new one, even though it was a logical decision to avoid loading the company with massive amounts of debt.

Still, the “new” 3M pays a dividend of about 1.8% based on today’s prices, representing about $1.55 billion per year (likely around $1.6 billion in 2026), and also does quite significant buybacks, guiding for another ~$2.5 billion in 2026, for a buyback yield of about 2.80%, which is an overall solid total yield, and it’s sustainable based on the adjusted FCF (again, doesn’t account for the litigation expenses).

Risks & Opportunities

Starting with the general factors such as the weak consumer environment, this actually makes 3M’s turnaround even more impressive, although the company is generally safer than others thanks to the kind of products they have.

As they show, the consumer discretionary segment was softer than expected, while the rest of the business helped drive a 2.7% increase in sales during the second half of the year despite it.

Still, what they aren’t safe from are higher costs of debt, which come as a consequence of the high-rate environment seen generally around the planet since the pandemic, although some regions are doing better than others in that regard, and 3M has the advantage of being able to tap into foreign capital at better rates if needed.

Meanwhile, competition (legal or not) can also remain an issue, which ultimately forces them into constantly spending high amounts for R&D just to remain relevant, which exposes them to yet another risk. We have to consider that with so many patents/product families and contracts, they can still face more lawsuits in the future, and the Combat Arms earplug settlements on top of the public water forever chemicals ones are just recent examples.

In fact, 3M is actively part of lawsuits of various sizes, such as the ongoing one for 3M’s Bair Hugger postsurgical warming blanket with over 8,600 active cases pending litigation, which ultimately highlights a fundamental risk when you have over 130,000 patents.

Meanwhile, especially with a big ~$2.6 billion payment for the forever chemicals in 2027 (~$3.4 billion in total litigation payments scheduled for that year), 3M can face significantly less flexibility in the near term, which can affect their ability to take advantage of potential opportunities, while the environment could then improve.

At the same time, the rising tensions between the US and Europe around Greenland - which were lowered to some degree following the framework announced after the Davos meeting - can still put pressure on the company's stock, although the impact of the tariffs themselves is not as big for a global giant with local operations like 3M (still a net exporter to Europe, though). The company's CEO also made a similar point during their Q4 Earnings Call, stating the estimated effect:

For us, the trade flows between U.S. and Europe is around $1 billion. We're a net exporter. So we export $700 million into Europe. We import back about $250 million. If you just run the numbers at 10% and then growing up to 25%, you can get to something in the order of $60 million, $70 million. But that's over the course of the year, we'll see some of that going in inventory. We'll see some of that dragging into 2027. So if that plays out exactly as we expect, the trade flow as I mentioned, it could be a $30 million, $40 million impact this year. But again, we're a long way from that becoming an executive order.

However, what it can cause is further pressure on their US and Europe operations, as that can still damage the market's trust and expectations, potentially leading to higher rates for longer, as the market has to price in the new risks and their effects, translating into higher treasury yields that impact their domestic borrowing costs.

Right now, we can see the yield on the 10-year notes still at relatively elevated levels despite the announcement. This can further affect the company's estimates and the final consumer, potentially causing them to miss the market's expectations - and we've just seen that the market can easily punish them for that following the crash on the day of the Q4 release.

On the bright side, these expenses should fall significantly after that, freeing up significant cash flow for 3M to potentially reinvest or return to shareholders through elevated dividends/buybacks.

Overall, it’s true that the new management turned the business around and has done well so far, but the stock already recovered over 80% from their lows from a couple of years ago, and I think most of the potential coming from this recovery/turnaround is already priced in.

This idea is also supported by one JPMorgan analyst, who also applauded the management’s above-expectations operational results, but noted that they see below-sector near-term earnings growth potential after this performance, saying:

While there is clearly momentum, we believe the early innings low-hanging fruit is behind in terms of the self help initiatives, solidly transitioning into the next phase which is reinvigorating the innovation engine, which we think will take time

This aligns with 3M’s value creation frameworks, as the company seems to have switched from the "easier" first steps that imply foundational changes away from a simple "holding" company and portfolio reshaping (with potential for more, of course) and is approaching the tougher part of actually delivering structurally higher growth and margins across their portfolio.

Risks Reiterated

Despite the overall solid performance since the new CEO took over, 3M’s relatively weak expectations from this point on (see quote above) combined with potential for more macro pressure and the timing of their litigation liabilities can ultimately result in weak results going forward, and we already saw the stock crash following the weaker-than-expected outlook for 2026.

Meanwhile, they still have to constantly innovate and spend significant amounts on R&D, while the timing of everything can result in lower shareholder returns in the near-future, and even though they’d be temporary, the market’s sentiment can shift just as fast as it improved.

Valuation

Starting with 3M's adjusted FCF of $4.37 billion in 2025, let's use S&P Global's estimates of $4.65 billion in 2026 and $4.72 billion in 2027, followed by a CAGR of 5% until 2030 thanks to their improvements and better macro conditions combined with potential debt repayments (below the market’s EPS expectations, for example) and a 3.5% CAGR until 2035, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate, a discount rate of 8.5%, and 539 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $81.82 billion, for an intrinsic value of $151.80, which is not far from the current $159.29 at the moment of writing this, while more optimistic expectations get us closer to it or even slightly above it, but nothing to get excited about.

Conclusions

I rate 3M a hold, as the stock seems to already reflect most of their turnaround, making the overall risk-reward less attractive, while they still face macro issues. They can certainly do better once again, but the overall upside from this level seems quite small overall.

data.title: Alpha Tau Medical: Pancreatic, GBM, And Other Updates; Set Up For A Strong, Catalyst-Rich 2026
data.description:
Overview

It has been an eventful start to the year for Alpha Tau Medical (DRTS), which I first wrote about back in October as a unique technology/mechanism and a story set to inflect over 2026 and beyond. The stock is up ~70% since my initiation and ~35% YTD (after two rather aggressive days of retracement). Some of the events/headlines driving the move over the last couple of months include:

  • Visibility: Participation in investor conferences in December (Piper Sandler and Sidoti) and then the JPM Healthcare Conference last week. Alpha Tau was and still largely is an underknown name to biotech investors—these conference appearances have begun to help change that. Average daily volume has increased from sub-100k shares in the summer/fall to ~400k over the past month or so.

  • December 2nd: Clearance from the FDA to begin a trial in prostate cancer, marking its fifth active indication in the US (link).

  • December 9th: Treatment of its first GBM patient (link).

  • January 5th: The announcement that the FDA had agreed to the use of a more flexible, modular (essentially rolling) submission process for Alpha DaRT in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC), in which they expect to report pivotal data in 2H26. (Link). I think investors have come to appreciate that Alpha Tau receives unique treatment from the FDA, including its standing quarterly meeting with the agency to discuss and update on its various programs.

  • January 6th: Announced the final results from its first-in-human pancreatic cancer trial that was conducted in Montreal (link). As I discussed in my initiation, this study was a bit of a hodgepodge of different treatment lines, disease stages, etc., but demonstrates impressive activity in severely undertreated pancreatic cancer, especially considering the trial used only incomplete tumor coverage (tumors were only partially covered with DaRTs). Most of this data had already been reported, though there were some interesting new biomarker data.

The company also provided some interesting color at the JPM Conference last week, including:

  • cSCC regulatory pathway: Dosing is expected to be completed in 1Q26, with a pivotal data readout in 2H26. It expects to submit for approval in 2H26, and given its Breakthrough Device Designation (BDD), hopes to potentially receive priority review, making an early 2027 approval plausible.

  • Pancreatic update: Patient demand has been very strong—enrollment/dosing is expected to be completed in 1H26, with initial results in 2H26.

  • GBM update: The first GBM patient was dosed, which went “fantastically” (refers to the successful placement of Alpha DaRTs, not efficacy outcomes), achieving 95% tumor coverage, and the patient was able to go home within 24 hours.

  • Japanese PMA: The company still expects an approval decision from the Japanese PMDA regarding Alpha DaRT in head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC).

I discuss more of management’s JPM commentary below, but the company appears to be on track or ahead of schedule for potential value-inflecting readouts and events in 2026.

There have also been some external/market factors that may be contributing to DRTS’ strong performance recently, namely:

  1. Rumors surrounding the acquisition of pancreatic-focused Revolution Medicines (RVMD).

  2. The success of alpha radiation biotech Aktis Oncology’s (AKTS) IPO.

In fact, the first question in the Q&A portion of Alpha Tau’s JPM presentation was about Revolution in pancreatic cancer and the implications for Alpha Tau in that indication. Management’s answer, which mirrored what I noted in my blurb/aside about Alpha Tau in my recent piece on Aktis, was that advancements in systemic therapies should only provide better combination therapy opportunities for Alpha DaRT, given its local application and very low toxicity profile (in stark contrast with most systemic therapies).

As I said in my 2025 Year-End Wrap-Up, Alpha Tau was one of my two or three favorite ideas coming into 2026, and while the stock move to start the year has eaten slightly into that return potential, the current ~$550 million market cap still appears quite attractive given Alpha DaRT’s unique positioning as the sole tumor-agnostic, localized application of alpha radiation in oncology, especially as we approach proof-of-concept US trial data in high-impact pancreatic and GBM indications in 2H26.

Pancreatic

For those following the Alpha Tau story, pancreatic and GBM have really become the two focal points, as both are severely undertreated.

At JPM, management reiterated that the ongoing PoC trial has been seeing “fantastic” interest from both patients and providers, primarily because of how few treatment options there are in the indication. Recall that patients are newly diagnosed with either locally advanced or metastatic disease and receive standard-of-care mFOLFIRINOX in combination with Alpha DaRT. According to ClinicalTrials, there are currently six actively recruiting sites in the US, including Emory University and NYU.

At JPM, the company stated that they expect to finish enrollment this quarter (essentially in line with prior guidance), with data expected in 2H26, after which the company intends to move quickly to apply to begin a pivotal trial.

Management did give some extra color regarding patients’ experience in the trial thus far, stating, “We have heard a lot of anecdotes about immediate pain relief following treatment,” which is significant as pancreatic cancer is known to involve intense localized pain in the abdomen.

As a reminder, the potential opportunity in pancreatic cancer is large (hence RevMed’s $23 billion valuation despite still being clinical-stage). The company estimates that 87% of the ~66k annual new patients have tumors that are unresectable.

The 30-patient readout in the 2H26 could be a significant catalyst, especially if it can compare favorably to something like Revolution Medicines’ daraxonrasib.

GBM

As stated above, the company enrolled its first GBM patient in early December at Ohio State University Medical Center.

CFO Raphi Levy stated in the JPM presentation that this patient’s procedure went “fantastically well,” achieving over 95% tumor coverage in a procedure that took ~1 hour, and that the patient was able to return home within 24 hours.

Taking a step back, it is pretty amazing that Alpha Tau was able to devise a way to apply Alpha DaRT precisely to patients’ brain tumors (the application technology is pictured below), an organ where, obviously, the surrounding tissue is extremely fragile and important to leave relatively undisturbed. That makes Alpha Tau’s achievement of 95% tumor coverage in a ~1-hour procedure and a sub-24-hour discharge extremely impressive.

The trial protocol dictates a cadence of one patient per month for the first three patients—the company says that the second patient is expected to be treated imminently (before the end of January)—after which enrollment will be allowed to increase and NYU will begin to enroll patients until they have enrolled all 10 patients. Management said during JPM that they expect to finish enrollment in 1H26, with data in 2H26, similar to pancreatic.

While things seem to be progressing well, the efficacy data could be a real potential catalyst for the stock. While speculation is cheap, if the company continues to be able to achieve ~95% tumor coverage, data in other cancer types suggests Alpha DaRT could be a potential breakthrough modality in GBM (in which it also happens to have Breakthrough Device Designation).

HNSCC

In my DRTS initiation piece, I discussed the impressive data generated from a small trial of Alpha DaRT in combination with Keytruda in recurrent or metastatic HNSCC. While there was no new data reported by the company at JPM, management’s discussion of the efficacy data during their presentation reminded me that a partnership with a larger pharma company (one that has a checkpoint inhibitor) would make a lot of sense in HNSCC and could be something that we see in 2026. Right now, Alpha Tau is still in a holding pattern with HNSCC, prioritizing other indications.

Additionally, Alpha Tau is still awaiting a potential PMDA approval in Japan for Alpha DaRT in HNSCC. Assuming this approval is achieved (it doesn’t feel completely certain given how much the timeline has been pushed back over the past several quarters), Alpha Tau plans to offer Alpha DaRT to some patients in Japan so as to satisfy Japanese regulators’ desire for post-approval patient data, though the company still plans to hold off on a full commercial launch until it is approved in the US.

2026 Catalysts and Regulatory Pathway

Alpha Tau’s playbook is clear: run small (n=10-30), quick, proof-of-concept trials in various indications (pancreatic, GBM, soon to be prostate) to demonstrate its impressive tumor-agnostic efficacy before applying to the FDA to begin a pivotal trial.

This strategy, along with its more mature cSCC program, is shaping up for an eventful 2026. The trials are enrolling as fast or faster than expected, and the FDA is clearly demonstrating an interest in allowing the company an expeditious path forward.

To reiterate, in 2026, we can expect:

  1. HNSCC approval in Japan (still) expected imminently.

  2. Final pivotal data in cSCC in 2H26, with a PMA submission and potential for priority review shortly thereafter. It seems likely that, even in the event of approval, the company would hold off on commercialization, based on prior comments about wanting to prioritize higher-impact underserved conditions (i.e., pancreatic, GBM).

  3. 30-patient pancreatic cancer data in 2H26 and potential advancement into a pivotal trial.

  4. 10-patient GBM data in 2H26 and potential advancement into a pivotal trial.

  5. Potential for a partnership of some sort with a large pharma, potentially in GBM, especially as the company gains more notoriety (and market cap) and reports more data in various indications.

(Based on management’s commentary, this graphic appears to be outdated with regard to GBM, which they state they expect to have initial data for in 2H26).

The pancreatic and GBM data in 2H26 represent major potential stock-moving catalysts.

Cash Position

The company should have adequate cash to comfortably get through all of these near- and medium-term catalysts, with $76 million of cash as of 9/30/25 and burning just $5-6 million per quarter (though this could increase a hair with the pancreatic and GBM trials in full swing, though both are small).

Revolution Medicines (RVMD)

Rumors have swirled over the past couple of weeks about both AbbVie and Merck potentially being in talks to acquire Revolution Medicines, which is developing a portfolio of RAS inhibitors for pancreatic cancer (and other cancers). This may have played a role in generating some attention for Alpha Tau, which concurrently put out a headline about its pancreatic trial data.

Interestingly, 90%+ of pancreatic cancer is driven by RAS mutations, meaning Revolution’s small molecule RAS inhibitor drugs are expected to treat almost all pancreatic cancer patients. To date, Revolution has reported data across different lines of treatment that suggest daraxonrasib, its lead candidate, will provide meaningful response and survival improvements over currently available treatments.

The first audience question at Alpha Tau’s JPM presentation asked about Revolution and what the approval of its RAS inhibitors could mean for Alpha Tau. CFO Raphi Levy’s response mirrored the thoughts I detailed in my Aktis Oncology piece, which is that Alpha DaRT’s tumor-agnostic mechanism and superb tolerability profile make it an ideal combination therapy for new systemic treatments, including RAS inhibitors like RevMed’s. If both Revolution and Alpha Tau are providing meaningful, yet still very far from universally curative, improvements versus SoC in pancreatic cancer, the combination of the two could have an additive or perhaps synergistic effect that moves us closer to actually providing lasting remission for more patients.

Revolution Medicines’ current market capitalization is $23 billion, which speaks to the valuation potential for oncology companies viewed as representing a breakthrough/paradigm shift.

Aktis IPO

Though I already touched on it in my recent piece on Aktis Oncology, I think the same dynamic applies to Aktis’ aspirations of utilizing systemic alpha-emitting radiopharmaceuticals to target specific tumor antigens.

While perhaps there is some extremely small chance that Aktis’ Nectin-4-targeting alpha radiopharmaceutical is so effective that it obviates the need for combination therapies, it seems much more likely that it, if successful, will move the efficacy frontier forward in various Nectin-4-expressing tumors (including GBM), but will still benefit from combination with a powerful, localized, low-side-effect treatment like Alpha DaRT (that has also shown to have immune-sensitizing effects).

Aktis’ current market cap is $1 billion, representing a ~75% premium to Alpha Tau. It is worth noting that while Aktis’ early imaging data suggest on-target activity, it has yet to report any actual efficacy data in humans.

Conclusion

Alpha Tau is a unique combination of

  1. A completely differentiated mechanism of action

  2. Situated within or adjacent to a “hot” space (radiopharmaceuticals, especially alpha particles)

  3. With a (very) large potential market opportunity (as a tumor-agnostic)

  4. And multiple potentially stock-moving readouts are expected over the next 6-12 months

2026 will likely mark Alpha Tau’s coming-out party in hindsight. At ~$550 million of market cap, I wouldn’t be surprised if Alpha Tau was up another 50-100% by the end of the year.

data.title: Top 5 Mining Stocks To Watch In 2026: No. 2 - Aris Mining
data.description:
The year 2026 is here, and it is time for the traditional "Top 5 Mining Stocks to Watch" list. 2025 was a huge success, as the record-breaking metals prices pushed the featured companies to triple-digit returns. As of December 29, Aris Mining (ARMN) was up 360%, followed by Taseko Mines (TGB) (186%), and Eldorado Gold (EGO) (141%). In the case of Calibre Mining, the calculation is a little more complex, as in June, the company was acquired by Equinox Gold (EQX). However, taking into account the 2024 closing price of Calibre, and the current value of 0.31 Equinox shares, Calibre is technically nearly 102% up. The least successful pick was Franco-Nevada (FNV), although a 78% gain can hardly be taken as a failure. On average, the 2025 Top 5 list gained 191.4%, the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) (169%), the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) (154%), the SPDR S&P Metals and Mining ETF (XME) (86%), and also the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) (17%).

It is not realistic to expect similar gains also in 2026, as the gold, silver, and copper prices will most probably ease the pace of growth (and possibly even give up a portion of the 2025 gains), however, numerous mining companies expect some major catalysts and have a good probability of outperforming their peers. Just like every year, I know that there are definitely some other companies with high potential that could have been considered for inclusion in the Top 5. If you are missing such a company on the list, please feel free to mention it in the comments section.

The fifth place on the 2026 list was captured by NexGold Mining (NXGCF), the fourth place was captured by U.S. Gold Corp. (USAU), and the third place was captured by Taseko Mines. Position No. 2 belongs to Aris Mining. Aris was also No. 2 on the 2025 list, and it recorded great returns. However, it entered the year 2025 significantly undervalued, and given the steep gold price growth, and the progress achieved at its development projects, Aris is undervalued also today. It is hard to expect nearly 400% gains also in 2026; however, triple-digit gains should be achievable quite easily if everything goes well.

The Current State of Aris Mining

Aris is operating two mines in Colombia. At the Segovia mine, a second mill was commissioned in June 2025. Segovia is expected to produce around 265,000-300,000 toz gold in 2026, as the ramp-up to the projected full capacity of 300,000 to gold per year continues. In 2025, the mine produced 231,177 toz gold. The current reserves of 1.5 million toz gold should suffice for the next 5 years of operations; however, the measured, indicated, and inferred resources contain nearly 6.5 million toz gold, which means that only less than 1/4 of the resources have been converted into reserves so far. Moreover, Aris keeps on drilling and discovering more gold at Segovia, so the actual mine life will end up significantly longer than the currently projected 5 years.

The Marmato mine produced only 28,741 toz gold in 2025; however, the small-scale operation is about to be expanded significantly. The Marmato bulk mining zone operation is under construction, which should be completed during the summer. The targeted full capacity of around 200,000 toz gold per year should be reached by the end of 2027. However, the first fruits of the expansion should be seen in H2 2026, and Marmato is targeted to produce 35,000-50,000 toz gold this year. The current reserves of 3.18 million toz gold should suffice for approximately 15 years. However, the reserves represent only around 36% of the currently known resources. It means that, similar to Segovia, the Marmato mine life will almost surely be expanded significantly.

Besides the two operating mines, there are also two advanced-stage projects. The more advanced one, Toroparu, is located in Guayana. Its measured and indicated resources contain 5.31 million toz gold, and inferred resources contain a further 1.18 million toz gold. Toroparu is fully permitted, and its previous owner has even initiated the early construction activities. However, after acquiring the project, Aris decided to stop the works and optimize the project. The result is a PEA that envisions an average annual production of 235,000 toz gold at an AISC of $1,289-toz, over a 21-year mine life. The initial CAPEX was projected at $830 million. Toroparu has an after-tax NPV (5%) of $1.8 billion at the base-case gold price of $3,000/toz. At a gold price of $4,000/toz, it is $3.24 billion. A PFS for Toroparu should be completed by H2 2026, and the construction decision should follow shortly after.

And there is also the Soto Norte project in Colombia. Aris consolidated its ownership to 100% only recently. The project contains reserves of 4.57 million toz gold, which represents only around 42% of the currently known resources. According to the PFS released in September, the mine should be producing around 203,000 toz gold per year at an AISC of only $534/toz, over a 22-year mine life. The AISC is so low due to meaningful copper by-product credits. Moreover, over the first 10 years, the production should average 263,000 toz gold per year. The initial CAPEX was projected at $625 million, which is a very reasonable price to develop a mining operation of this size. Therefore, the economics of the project are very attractive. The after-tax NPV (5%) is estimated at $3.27 billion, at a gold price of $3,000/toz. However, it is as much as $4.75 billion at a gold price of $4,000/toz.

It is also important to mention Aris Mining's highly experienced management team. Worth mentioning is especially the CEO of the company, Neil Woodyer. He is the former founder and CEO of Endeavour Mining and Leagold Mining. And as Aris Mining's Chair of the Board of Directors, Ian Telfer, the former CEO of Goldcorp, and the former Chairman of the World Gold Council, serves. He is also a member of the Canadian Business Hall of Fame, as well as the Canadian Mining Hall of Fame.

The 2026 Catalysts

Several catalysts are expected in 2026. First of all, the Segovia mine ramp-up continues, and production of 265,000-300,000 toz gold is expected in 2026. Marmato bulk zone should start ramping up in H2 2026. The full capacity of 200,000 toz gold per year should be reached by 2027. In 2026, 35,000-50,000 toz gold is expected from Marmato. It means that if everything goes well, Aris Mining should produce 300,000-350,000 toz gold in 2026.

In Q3 2025, Aris produced 73,236 toz gold and generated an operating cash flow of $105.6 million, at an average realized gold price of $3,494/toz. It means that at a gold price of $3,500/toz, production of 325,000 toz gold should lead to an operating cash flow of $470 million. At a gold price of $4,000/toz, it should be closer to $600 million (of course, these calculations are simplified, based on an extrapolation of the Q3 2025 financial results). The median price-to-operating cash flow ratio in the mining industry is 15.96 right now. Using a more conservative value of 10 leads to a market capitalization of $6 billion. This is 50% more than Aris Mining's current market capitalization of $4 billion. Moreover, this calculation is based on a gold price of $4,000/toz, which looks relatively conservative, as the current market price is around $4,800/toz. It means that Segovia and Marmato alone should provide significant upside at the current gold prices. Any news confirming that the Segovia and Marmato operations are on track to reach the guidance should serve as positive catalysts.

Moreover, in H2 2026, the PFS for the Toroparu project should be released. It is possible to expect some improvements to the economics of the project. The company plans to move to the construction phase after the PFS is completed. Given that Toroparu is fully permitted, this may mean that the construction will start in early 2027. However, more clarity regarding the schedule should be known after the PFS is released. A schedule envisaging relatively rapid construction and ramp-up can serve as another catalyst.

And another catalyst, expected sometime in H1 2026, is the application for an environmental license for Soto Norte.

Conclusion

Of course, there are also risks. First of all, it is the gold price. It is at record highs, in the $4,800/toz area. Only a year ago, such a high price was hard to imagine. A correction will come sooner or later, and it will have a negative short-term impact on the share price of Aris Mining. However, if it stabilizes at a reasonable level, say in the $3,500-4,000 area, Aris will still have a triple-digit upside potential compared to its current share price.

In the case of Aris, the jurisdiction risk must also be taken into account. Segovia, Marmato, and Soto Norte are located in Colombia, which is a country well known for security issues. Toroparu is located in Guyana, and it will provide some geographical diversification, but not much. A sizeable operation in a safer country, like the USA, Canada, or Australia, would be a nice addition.

There is also the permitting risk related to Soto Norte and, obviously, also operational risks related to the Marmato bulk zone ramp-up, Toroparu construction and ramp-up, and later also Soto Norte construction and ramp-up. What is positive, Aris is in a very good financial condition, with a cash of $390 million as of the end of 2025, and debt of $518 million as of the end of Q3. At the current gold prices, there is a good chance to finance Toroparu and Soto Norte constructions from cash flows and debt, avoiding share dilution completely, or limiting it to a very reasonable level.

However, the risks are outweighed by the significant upside potential. As calculated above, it is possible to expect an operating cash flow of around $600 million in 2026, at a gold price of $4,000/toz. This should warrant at least a $6 billion market capitalization, or a share price of around $30, which is 50% above its current level. However, this calculation doesn't reflect the fact that the Marmato bulk zone should push Aris Mining's production level to 500,000 toz gold per year by the end of 2027. And it doesn't attribute any value to Toroparu and Soto Norte, which have a combined after-tax NPV (5%) of $8 billion at a gold price of $4,000/toz.

In other words, if everything goes according to plan, Aris Mining's share price may double quite easily before the end of 2026, even if the gold price calms down and consolidates around the $4,000/toz level or slightly lower.
